title: Curse of the Mummy's Tomb (Games Workshop) - the solo mode is basically Candyland
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kCcXFPN8bK0

Daniel (@dungeondive) revisits Curse of the Mummy's Tomb, a 1988 Games Workshop game he keeps purely for the art — much of it by the great Gary Chalk, the Lone Wolf and GW stalwart he's championed many times. As a solo game, he says, it's "basically Candyland" and one of the worst he owns: you draw movement cards and advance up a three-level pyramid board, and once the deck is shuffled the outcome is essentially decided. There are no real decisions — you can be one space and one stat-roll from winning and stall there forever, since victory means rolling three starred attributes at or under on a d12.

Daniel walks through a sample solo turn: rolling for the stalking mummy, drawing movement, and resolving numbered spaces by facing a hazard, a creature, and a discovery in order, with combat as simple opposed d12 rolls. He pulls out the flavour-text sheets — vintage GW atmosphere at its best — and sketches house rules (press-your-luck card draws, a Talisman-style hand) that might inject a little tension. The competitive mode, where the movement and encounter decks combine into hands you can weaponise against opponents, is clearly the intended game and far better. His advice: a collector's piece for Chalk and 80s GW fans only, and never at eBay's inflated prices.

----

Daniel keeps Curse of the Mummy's Tomb on the shelf for Gary Chalk's art alone, despite a solo mode he calls Candyland. Is there an artist whose work would make you hold onto a game you'd otherwise never play?
